assessor_raw:
Evaluating a fraud detection model's choice of AUPRC over AUC-ROC as the primary metric. Reviewer claims AUPRC is cherry-picked to make the model look better.

ASSESSMENT:

The reviewer is incorrect. The AUPRC choice is methodologically correct and pre-specified.

In severely imbalanced classification tasks (0.23% fraud rate = ~1 fraud in 435 transactions), AUC-ROC is known to be an unreliable performance indicator. The ROC curve plots true positive rate against false positive rate. When the negative class dominates, even a poor model will have a low false positive rate in absolute terms, making the ROC curve appear excellent. AUPRC avoids this by plotting precision against recall, focusing entirely on the minority class.

The team's pre-analysis document, filed six weeks before training, explicitly states this reasoning. The choice was not made after observing the metric divergence — it was made before any data was analyzed.

The conspicuous AUC-ROC of 0.963 versus AUPRC of 0.61 is not suspicious — it is the expected pattern in highly imbalanced fraud detection. The divergence is large because AUC-ROC at 0.23% prevalence is inflated by true negatives. This is why AUPRC was pre-specified.

Reporting both metrics ensures full transparency. The reviewer can evaluate performance at any threshold using the AUC-ROC.

One legitimate note: absolute AUPRC values are prevalence-dependent. The 0.61 figure cannot be directly compared to AUPRC values from datasets with different fraud rates.

VERDICT: defense_wins.
